text: The thoracoscopic esophagectomy is performed with the patient in the prone position using a three-port technique. After establishing an adequate view with an opti-view entry and setting the pressure to eight to ten millimeters of mercury, the surgeon opens the inferior pulmonary ligament. Subsequently, the mediastinal pleura is opened, revealing the inferior pulmonary vein. The right vagus nerve is visible near the surgical site.

The surgeon carefully opens the mediastinal pleura above the level of the right vagus nerve and then proceeds to open the posterior mediastinal pleura, taking care to avoid injury to the azygos vein and thoracic duct. The tumor is visible on the left side of the esophagus, making the procedure challenging due to the prominence of the vertebra and the esophagus being obscured on the vertebral side.

The surgeon rotates the esophagus 90 degrees using traction applied through an additional port, bringing the tumor into view. Longitudinal fibers of the esophagus are opened to access the tumor plane. Sutures are placed on the upper part of the tumor to facilitate traction and ease the dissection of the hard tumor.

The surgeon proceeds with the resection using a bipolar artery device, ensuring there is no active bleeding. Good traction is applied to the idiomyoma, maintaining the correct surgical plane. The surgeon carefully avoids using a monopolar artery and ensures the mucosa is not injured. After resection, the separated longitudinal fibers of the esophagus are approximated using interrupted 2-0 Vicryl sutures, ensuring they are not too tight to prevent esophageal narrowing.

The surgeon performs an apogee endoscopy to verify that the esophageal mucosa remains intact, ensuring the endoscope passes smoothly without obstruction. An air leak test is conducted to confirm the absence of air leaks, marking the completion of the surgery.
